shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
During her photo session at the rescue, Ladybug apparently made a great impression. That tracks, because she also makes a point of sticking her paw out mid-cuddle session to make sure she's not being left out. She's two months old, about two pounds, and already very clear about what she wants: attention, snuggles, and to be included.

She came into Mostly Mutts from animal control with her siblings, spent time in a foster home, and recently moved to the Kitty Aquarium at the shelter. She got along well with the adult cats in her foster home and is fully litter trained, which is not a given at this age. She's playful and social, the kind of kitten who's going to figure out your routine fast and insert herself into it.

She's already microchipped and has started her kitten shots. You'll need to finish those out as she grows, and her spay is required and will be completed when she's big enough. She's not there yet, but she's getting there fast.

Mostly Mutts is based in Kennesaw. Call or email to set up a visit and meet Ladybug in person.
scoring_notes:
• Two-month-old kitten with a paw-out-during-cuddles personality detail is instantly shareable and visually compelling for print
• Already litter trained at this age and social with adult cats removes the most common kitten adoption hesitations upfront
• Recently moved to the shelter floor signals she is newly available and likely to move fast, giving the feature a timely angle

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Iceman was adopted from Mostly Mutts as a kitten and spent five years with his family until their situation changed. Now he's back, five years old, 11 pounds, and ready to find his people again. He's been through the whole adoption process before, so he knows how this goes. He just needs someone to choose him.

He's described as sweet and handsome, which, fair enough, but what stands out more is how he spends his time: hanging out with his people and watching the world go by. No drama, no chaos. Just a calm, affectionate cat who wants to be near you. He was also a hit with four kids in a previous foster home, so if you've got children at home, there's a solid track record there.

One thing worth knowing: he didn't love other male cats in his previous home. A female cat companion would probably be fine, and the rescue is open to meet and greets to see how things go. He comes fully vetted, up to date on shots, neutered, microchipped, and combo tested negative. Adoption fee is $175.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Call or email to get the process started.
scoring_notes:
• Five years of family life cut short through no fault of his own is an emotionally resonant story that drives reader empathy
• Calm, affectionate personality with a proven track record around children makes him broadly appealing to newsletter families
• Returning to the adoption process after half a decade creates genuine urgency and a second-chance narrative readers want to resolve

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
She came in as part of a litter the rescue named after the Go-Gos, got adopted, and then came back through no fault of her own. Her name is Gina, she's about 11 months old, and she weighs five pounds. She will probably always weigh five pounds. If you've ever wanted a cat that never quite loses that kitten energy or kitten size, she is genuinely that cat.

Her foster calls her the best cat ever, and the details back that up. She's independent but loves to snuggle, got along great with the adult cats in her foster home, wasn't bothered by a dog on a leash, and liked the kids she's met. She's playful and sweet and doesn't have a complicated personality.

Here's the honest part: Gina has some ongoing stomach issues. She occasionally vomits, and despite a lot of vet care and food changes, nobody has pinned down a definitive cause. She's improved a lot, and she's otherwise completely healthy and happy. Because she's on a special diet, she'd do best as your only cat or in a home where feeding separately is doable. That's a pretty manageable ask for the right person.

Mostly Mutts is based in Kennesaw. Reach out by phone or email to start the process and arrange a meet with Gina.
scoring_notes:
• Perpetual kitten size and energy is a rare, highly marketable trait that will resonate with readers
• Return adoption after a litter named after the Go-Gos gives her a charming, memorable backstory with emotional hook
• Manageable medical caveat and second shelter stint signal a pet in genuine need of the right reader to step up

blurb:
Jacquelyn came in from animal control and landed in a foster home where she has been thriving ever since. She's about two years old, 58 pounds, and her foster describes her as lovable, huggable, and genuinely fun to be around. That tracks. She's the kind of dog who wiggles her way into your lap not because she's pushy, but because she just really wants to be close to her people.

She's a total Velcro dog and loves having someone home with her during the day. She does need a few potty breaks, but her foster says no more than her own dogs need, so nothing unusual there. She's been great through the night too. She does have a congenital kidney defect, which is managed easily and doesn't slow her down at all. It does mean her lifespan may be a little shorter than average, but she has no idea and is fully living her best life.

She loves other dogs and would do great with a friendly dog at home. Cats are a no, she's a little too curious. Kids are a maybe, she just hasn't been around them much, but older kids would likely be a good fit. She's also an excellent alert dog, so if you want to know the moment anyone walks past your house, she has you covered.

Jacquelyn is available through Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue in Kennesaw. Submit an inquiry on their website to get started.
scoring_notes:
• A congenital kidney condition that does not slow her down at all is a powerful love-her-anyway narrative that drives deep reader connection
• Velcro dog personality paired with the alert-dog detail gives the blurb both warmth and a practical hook for the right household
• Animal control origin combined with health complexity suggests a longer road to adoption, raising the stakes and urgency for the feature

blurb:
Yasmeen is eight years old and she has already done the hard work of becoming a really good dog. She went through a prison foster and training program after Mostly Mutts pulled her from animal control, and she graduated knowing exactly how to build trust with people. Her foster mom says adopters will hit the jackpot with her, and based on everything in her profile, that's not an exaggeration.

She's loyal and loving once she knows you, which usually happens fast. In the training program it took about a day. She does like to keep an eye on things when a stranger comes into her home, but the trainers have found a simple fix: introduce new people outside first, on neutral ground, and she adjusts well. That's a manageable ask for a dog this devoted to her people.

She's good with cats and has done well meeting other dogs, though she comes across as a little dominant at first before settling into play mode. For kids, she does best with dog-savvy teens 15 and up, and her trainer would want to do a meet. She's not a complicated dog, she just wants to trust you, and once she does, she's all in.

Yasmeen is in a foster home now and available through Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. Visit their website to start the inquiry process and set up a time to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Prison foster and training program graduation is an exceptional backstory with built-in human interest that elevates the entire newsletter feature
• Eight years old with a complex trust profile and specific household requirements means she faces a significantly narrower adoption window, making urgency real
• Trainer-backed insight that she becomes fully devoted within a day reframes her as a deeply rewarding adopt for the reader willing to meet her halfway

blurb:
Josie has floppy ears, a nubby little tail, and enough energy to outlast most people on a Saturday. She's a six-year-old hound mix, about 35 pounds, and she has been waiting for the right match. She was adopted once before and returned because the energy level wasn't a fit for that family. That's not a knock on Josie. That's just Josie being honest about what she needs.

Her foster puts her energy at a 7 or 8 out of 10, which is real. She wants to run, play, and go on actual adventures with you. The good news is that when the day is done, she flips completely into cuddle mode and will happily melt into the couch with you. High energy and high affection is a pretty good combo if you're the right person for it.

She does best as an only dog and shouldn't live with cats. She hasn't spent much time around kids yet, but her foster thinks older kids around 10 and up could be a great match since she loves to play. She does some mouthy play, never mean, just enthusiastic, which is worth knowing going in. She graduated from the Mostly Mutts training program, so she has a solid foundation to build on.

Josie is currently in a foster home and available through Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. Head to their website to submit an inquiry and go meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Returned for energy mismatch, not behavior, is an honest and relatable framing that reframes the return as a matchmaking problem readers can solve
• High-energy adventure dog who fully melts into the couch at night is a lifestyle pitch that will land with an active reader demographic
• A prior return plus specific household requirements signal she has been waiting longer than average, adding real urgency to feature her now
